reasoning: Verified every step against the code. (1) Backend stays healthy with dead TTS: pyttsx3 import failure (python/main.py:64-69) only logs; TTSEngine.__init__ returns early (672-673) with engine=None/_queue=None; pyttsx3.init() failure on the worker thread (703-705) leaves engine=None while the worker drains the queue logging 'TTS not available. Would speak:' (719); a runAndWait wedge (717) blocks the worker while speak() (733-735) keeps returning instantly. (2) No health signal: _speak (2560-2566) sends nothing back to the client â€” it even logs the utterance as spoken via _save_chat_message; grep confirms no tts_available/status message anywhere; useWebSocket.tsx has no TTS-health handling and ws.speak (line 608) is fire-and-forget. (3) Commit b097efb changed handleSpeak (App.tsx:243-250) from unconditional browser speakText + optional ws.speak to an either/or keyed solely on ws.isConnected, so with the socket up and TTS dead the browser voice never fires. (4) Both emergency paths (PersistentEmergencyButton App.tsx:100-112, GlobalNavBar.tsx:195-207) route through handleSpeak, so the patient is silently mute on EMERGENCY while the header shows green 'Connected' (App.tsx:429). (5) It is a true regression: pre-commit, browser SpeechSynthesis fired on every utterance, so a broken backend TTS still produced audio. On manifestation: routine fault-free operation won't hit it, but the triggering fault is realistically reachable in normal patient use â€” TTSEngine.stop() (main.py:759-763) calls engine.stop() cross-thread against a worker-owned engine whose thread affinity the code itself documents (line 687), and STOP buttons are part of normal gaze UI (WebBrowsingScreen.tsx:1242, 2521); pyttsx3's runAndWait hang after external stop is a well-documented Windows/SAPI5 failure mode. When (not if, over months of 24/7 AAC use) the engine wedges or the venv dependency drifts, every utterance including emergency is silently swallowed with zero on-screen indication and no recovery short of restarting the backend â€” a total-mute failure for an ALS patient. Only nit: the report's line numbers drift slightly (worker no-op log is line 719, not 738), but the cited code exists exactly as described.
suggestedFix: Make the fallback condition reflect TTS health, not socket health, and add an ack channel. Backend: (a) TTSEngine exposes an `available` flag â€” set True only after _init_engine succeeds (have _worker_loop report init result via a threading.Event or status field), False on init failure or when TTS_AVAILABLE is False; (b) include `tts_available` in the initial status/connected payload and whenever it changes; (c) in the 'speak' handler (main.py:1860 / _speak at 2560), reply to the client with e.g. {type:'speak_result', ok: self.tts.available} so the frontend can detect silent no-ops; optionally add a queue-depth/last-utterance watchdog to flag a wedged runAndWait worker and flip tts_available to False. Frontend: handleSpeak (src/App.tsx:243) becomes `if (ws.isConnected && ws.ttsAvailable) ws.speak(text) else speakText(...)`, with useWebSocket tracking ttsAvailable from the status/speak_result messages (default false until the backend confirms, so a connected-but-TTS-dead backend immediately falls back to browser SpeechSynthesis). Separately (pre-existing but compounding): route TTSEngine.stop() through the worker queue instead of calling engine.stop() cross-thread, to remove the most likely real-world wedge trigger.
